data.title: Granite secures $495M federal contract for LRT-4 Webb-Zapata project
David Sacks steps aside as Trump’s AI and crypto czar
data.description:
Venture capitalist David Sacks is stepping down from his role as artificial intelligence and crypto czar for President Donald Trump after reaching the 130-day limit as a special government employee.

Sacks said he will transition to the President’s Council of Advisers on Science & Technology ((PCAST)), where he will serve as co-chair and advise on a broader range of technology issues beyond AI.

“I think moving forward as co-chair of PCAST, I can now make recommendations on not just AI but an expanded range of technology topics,” he said.

PCAST is a federal advisory group made up of industry and academic experts that provides guidance to the president on technology, scientific research, and innovation policy.

Sacks has been a key voice in the White House during Trump’s second term and is a longtime Silicon Valley investor and co-founder of Craft Ventures. He is part of the so-called "PayPal Mafia," referring to former PayPal (PYPL) staff and founders who went on to start other tech companies. The group includes Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk and Palantir (PLTR) chairman Peter Thiel.

Sacks said he will continue to support the administration’s AI framework released last week, which focuses in part on easing permitting and power generation to enable faster infrastructure buildouts without increasing residential electricity costs.

A White House memo last March also showed Sacks sold more than $200M in digital asset-related investments.

Here are some tech-related ETFs: (NYSEARCA:VGT), (NYSEARCA:IYW), (NYSEARCA:FTEC), (NYSEARCA:IXN), and (NYSEARCA:RSPT).

Artificial Intelligence: (NASDAQ:AIQ), (NYSEARCA:DTEC), (NYSEARCA:GINN), (NYSEARCA:TECB), (NASDAQ:XT), (NYSEARCA:THNQ), and (NYSEARCA:CHAT).

data.title: Rapid7 bolsters command platform with Kenzo Security acquisition
Asia markets wobble as Trump delays Iran energy strikes; Tehran rejects 'one-sided' truce
data.description:
Asian equity markets traded mixed on Friday as investors remained cautious amid conflicting reports on the Middle East

In its latest development, US President Donald Trump announced he would extend a pause on attacks against Iran’s energy infrastructure into April and described talks with Tehran as ‘going very well.’ However, an Iranian official dismissed a reported US proposal to end the conflict as “one-sided and unfair.” 

WTI crude futures fell toward $93 per barrel on Friday, trimming gains from the previous session.

Gold prices rose above $4,400 per ounce on Friday after a sharp decline in the previous session.

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 0.18% to below 52,700, while the broader Topix Index dropped 0.9% to 3,610 on Friday. The Japanese yen held its recent decline to around 159.5 per dollar on Friday, close to the critical 160 level.

China (SHCOMP) stock markets rose on Friday, with the Shanghai index up 0.75% and the Shenzhen Component gaining 0.4%, driven by positive economic data. The offshore yuan remained weak, trading at 6.91 per dollar. 

Markets are watching for upcoming US–China talks, with Trump set to meet Xi Jinping in May. Industrial profits in China surged 15.2% year-on-year to CNY 1.02 trillion in early 2026.

Hong Kong (HSI) rose 0.82% to around 24,753 on Friday, extending losses from the previous session.

India (SENSEX) fell 1.45% to around 74,335 in early trade on Friday, reversing the gains of the past two sessions. The Indian rupee strengthened to around 94.1 per dollar, recovering from a record low.

Australia (AS51) fell 0.11% and edged down on Friday, finishing at 8,516 and marking the second straight session of losses. The Australian dollar weakened to around $0.687 on Friday, hitting a two-month low.

Separately, the OECD warned Australia could face one of the highest inflation rates among advanced economies and severe fertilizer supply disruptions amid geopolitical risks. 

In the U.S. on Thursday, all three major indexes ended lower as a sharp spike in energy prices and hardening geopolitical tensions intensified stagflation fears.

U.S. stock futures rose on Friday, recovering some losses from the previous session: Dow +0.53%; S&P 500 +0.61%; Nasdaq +0.67%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

data.title: Anthropic wins court order blocking U.S. ban on its AI technology
data.description:
Anthropic PBC (ANTHRO) won a court order blocking a Trump administration ban on government use of its artificial intelligence technology, after arguing the move could cost the company billions in lost revenue.

U.S. District Judge Rita F. Lin issued a preliminary injunction on Thursday, halting the administration’s plan to cut ties with Anthropic while the case proceeds in San Francisco federal court. The order was stayed for seven days to allow the government time to appeal.

The dispute stems from a Defense Department determination earlier this month that Anthropic posed a threat to the U.S. supply chain, escalating tensions over how the military can use AI tools. Anthropic (ANTHRO) had sought assurances its technology would not be used for mass surveillance or autonomous weapons deployment, while the government cited national security concerns and rejected any restrictions.

In her ruling, Lin questioned the basis for the ban, saying it did not appear to serve national security interests. She suggested the measures were aimed at penalizing the company, calling them “classic illegal First Amendment retaliation.”

Anthropic (ANTHRO) said it welcomed the decision, adding it remains focused on “working productively with the government to ensure all Americans benefit from safe, reliable AI.”

The company argues it was excluded from government contracts for its stance on AI safeguards, while the administration has signaled it will continue to pursue efforts to remove Anthropic from federal agencies. Government lawyers said trust is critical in military partnerships and accused Anthropic of undermining that trust during negotiations.

The case also extends to a parallel challenge filed in a Washington, D.C., appellate court, where Anthropic claims the Defense Department exceeded its authority with actions that were “arbitrary, capricious and an abuse of discretion.”

Anthropic (ANTHRO) signed a $200M contract with the United States Department of Defense in July, becoming the first lab to deploy its models in mission workflows on classified networks. Rivals OpenAI (OPENAI), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), and xAI (X.AI) also received DoD awards worth up to $200M last year.

In other news, Anthropic PBC (ANTHRO) is weighing an initial public offering as soon as October, as it competes with rival OpenAI (OPENAI) to go public.

data.title: Anthropic weighs IPO as soon as October amid race with OpenAI, report says
data.description:
Anthropic PBC (ANTHRO) is weighing an initial public offering as soon as October, Bloomberg reported, citing sources, as it competes with rival OpenAI (OPENAI) to go public.

The company has held early talks with Wall Street banks about leading roles on a potential listing, with Goldman Sachs (GS), JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Morgan Stanley (MS) among those expected to be considered.

A listing could raise more than $60B, according to The Information.

Founded in 2021 by former OpenAI staffers including chief executive officer Dario Amodei, Anthropic (ANTHRO) was valued at $380B in a $30B funding round co-led by MGX that closed in February. The company has partnerships with Alphabet’s Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT) and Nvidia (NVDA). These established firms have taken stakes in the AI startup, and have given Anthropic specialized chips and other technology in deals worth tens of billions of dollars.

Earlier this year, Anthropic (ANTHRO) was designated by the Pentagon as a threat to the U.S. supply chain under an authority typically reserved for foreign adversaries. However, the company secured a court order on Thursday blocking the restriction on government use of its technology, arguing the move could cost it billions in lost revenue.

Anthropic signed a $200M contract with the United States Department of Defense in July, becoming the first lab to deploy its models in mission workflows on classified networks. Rivals OpenAI (OPENAI), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), and xAI (X.AI) also received DoD awards worth up to $200M last year.

data.title: Bitmine Immersion Technologies acquires Pier Two to power MAVAN staking launch
Trump’s signature to appear on U.S. currency, a first for a sitting president
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump’s signature will appear on future U.S. paper currency to mark the country’s 250th anniversary, in what would be a first for a sitting president, according to the Treasury Department.

“There is no more powerful way to recognize the historic achievements of our great country and President Donald J. Trump than U.S. dollar bills bearing his name,” Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a statement.

U.S. currency has traditionally carried the signatures of the Treasury secretary and the treasurer.

The move comes as Trump has sought to shape the nation’s semiquincentennial celebrations this year, including plans to host a UFC fight at the White House.

Trump’s allies have also pushed to attach his name to major institutions while he remains in office, including the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts in Washington and an airport in Palm Beach, Florida.

Printing Trump’s signature on U.S. currency “is not only appropriate, but also well deserved,” U.S. Treasurer Brandon Beach said.

Beach added in October that the department was also considering minting a $1 coin featuring Trump’s image.

Dear readers, We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Trump to sign order to pay TSA workers during shutdown
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said he plans to sign an order to pay Transportation Security Administration ((TSA)) officers, aiming to ease airport disruptions as Congress remains deadlocked over ending a partial government shutdown.

In a social media post Thursday, “I am going to sign an Order instructing the Secretary of Homeland Security, Markwayne Mullin, to immediately pay our TSA Agents in order to address this Emergency Situation, and to quickly stop the Democrat Chaos at the Airports. It is not an easy thing to do, but I am going to do it!”

According to a senior administration official, the payments will be funded using money from Trump’s 2025 tax and spending bill. The White House used a similar approach last year to pay military personnel during a broader shutdown.

The move sidesteps the regular congressional funding process and could face legal challenges.

The action comes amid a weeks-long funding standoff over the Department of Homeland Security, which oversees the TSA, leading to staffing shortages and longer wait times at airport checkpoints.

Immigration and Customs Enforcement ((ICE)) agents have been deployed at airports to help ease delays, and ICE and border patrol personnel have continued to receive pay through the shutdown due to additional funding allocated in the July spending bill. However, other DHS workers, including TSA officers, have gone unpaid for weeks, disrupting travel at major hubs and adding pressure to an economy already dealing with higher energy prices linked to the Iran conflict.

Negotiations in Congress have intensified as lawmakers head into a two-week break. Democrats have held up DHS funding in a bid to secure changes to immigration policies following the killings of two US citizens by agents earlier this year, while Republicans have rejected proposals from Democrats for a standalone bill to fund TSA operations and parts of the DHS that are not related to immigration.

data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM International Trade in Goods (Advance)

This monthly report offers advance import and export data on the goods components of the monthly trade report.

10:00 AM Consumer Sentiment

The University of Michigan's Consumer Survey Center questions households each month on their assessment of current conditions and expectations of future conditions.

The consensus looks for sentiment to be revised down to 54.3 in the final March report from 55.5 in the preliminary March reading due largely to rising energy prices flowing from the Iran war.

11:00 AM Thomas Barkin Speaks 

Richmond Federal Reserve Bank President Thomas Barkin speaks before the Appalachian Highlands Economic Forum.

11:30 AM Mary Daly Speaks 

San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly gives introductory remarks before the Macroeconomics and Monetary Policy Conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco.

11:35 AM Anna Paulson Speaks

Philadelphia Federal Reserve Bank President Anna Paulson speaks before the Macroeconomics and Monetary Policy Conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco.

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs.
